title: Lesson 3 — NAND universality
_authoring: Pedagogical full pass (curriculum audit). Added intro with lessonIntro, per-KC explanations (nand-construction as primitive, nand-universality as the functional-completeness claim), and a worked example (build NOT from NAND: NAND(A,A) = NOT(A AND A) = NOT A). Reordered items: l3-nand-basic moved to tier 1 as the FIRST item — establish the NAND primitive before deriving NOT/AND/OR from it. l3-not-from-nand, l3-and-from-nand, l3-or-from-nand follow as tier 1 universality derivations. Tiers 2-4 unchanged structurally. All truthTables verified MSB-first.

lessonIntro.title: Lesson 3 — NAND and Universality
lessonIntro.body: NAND (Not-AND) seems like a minor variation of AND, but it turns out to be the only gate you ever need: every Boolean function — AND, OR, NOT, XOR, everything — can be built from NAND alone. This is called functional completeness, and understanding it changes how you read circuits. In this lesson you will first master the NAND truth table, then derive NOT, AND, and OR from NAND-only constructions.

explanations.body: NAND stands for NOT-AND: it is the complement of AND. NAND(A, B) = NOT(A AND B). The truth table is almost the inverse of AND — the only row where NAND outputs 0 is when both inputs are 1. Everything else is 1. To memorise it: NAND is generous (outputs 1 most of the time); AND is strict (outputs 1 only when both are 1).

Universality (functional completeness) means any Boolean function can be expressed using only NAND gates. The proof is constructive: show how to build NOT, AND, and OR from NAND, and since NOT/AND/OR can express everything, NAND can too. NOT A = NAND(A, A) — tie both inputs together. AND(A,B) = NOT(NAND(A,B)) = NAND(NAND(A,B), NAND(A,B)). OR(A,B) = NAND(NOT A, NOT B) = NAND(NAND(A,A), NAND(B,B)).

steps.label: Recall the NAND definition
steps.detail: NAND(A, B) = NOT(A AND B). The output is 0 only when both A and B are 1.

steps.label: Tie both NAND inputs to A
steps.detail: Compute NAND(A, A) = NOT(A AND A). Since A AND A = A (a value ANDed with itself is itself), we get NOT(A AND A) = NOT A.

steps.label: Verify with the truth table
steps.detail: NAND(0, 0) = NOT(0 AND 0) = NOT 0 = 1. NAND(1, 1) = NOT(1 AND 1) = NOT 1 = 0. Output column: [1, 0] — exactly the NOT truth table.

steps.label: Circuit interpretation
steps.detail: Take one NAND gate, connect both input wires to A. The output is NOT A. This is the simplest universality demonstration: one gate, both inputs tied, inverts the signal.

items.targetExpression: (A AND NOT B) OR (NOT A AND B)
(A AND B) OR (NOT A AND NOT B)
(A AND B) OR (B AND C) OR (A AND C)
(A AND C) OR (B AND NOT C)
